blocks.content: They stopped talking somewhere between the last booth and the Ferris wheel line.

Not because there was nothing to say. Because the silence that had arrived was not the kind you filled. It was the kind you moved through together — the weight of the afternoon, the weight of what was coming, walking with them as they walked.
The line was short. Late afternoon, the crowd thinning toward the exit and the food stands, leaving the rides to whoever had come for the view.
A sign at the entrance said: The view from the top encompasses the whole of Hartwell and the river beyond.
Cassandra read it. Then read it again, as though making sure.

Ethan was watching the wheel turn — the gondolas rising to the apex and pausing there, briefly, while the one below loaded, and then descending. Rising. Pausing. Descending. The patient geometry of it.
He was thinking about nothing. He had the particular emptiness of someone who has cleared space in themselves deliberately, made room for something.
The gondola arrived. The operator opened the bar.
The ground began, slowly, to fall away beneath them.
